content:
# Leviticus Chapter 5 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Sources:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic) | **Hebrew:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 59v–60r | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 89–90 (pa_0089–pa_0090)

---

**1** "If a person sins in that he hears a public adjuration to testify, and though he is a witness — whether he has seen or has come to know of it — yet does not speak up, he shall bear his iniquity.

**2** Or if a person touches any unclean thing — whether the carcass of an unclean wild animal, or a carcass of unclean livestock, or the carcass of unclean swarming creatures — even though it is hidden from him, and he is unclean and incurs guilt;

**3** or if he touches human uncleanness — any kind of uncleanness by which a person becomes unclean — and it is hidden from him, and he comes to know it, and he incurs guilt;

**4** or if a person swears, uttering rashly with his lips — whether to do evil or to do good — whatever it is that a man utters rashly in an oath, and it is hidden from him, and he comes to know it, and he incurs guilt in any one of these —

**5** when he incurs guilt in any of these things, he shall confess the sin he has committed.

**6** And he shall bring his guilt payment to the LORD for the sin that he has committed: a female from the flock — a lamb or a female goat — as a sin offering. And the priest shall make atonement for him for his sin.

**7** But if he cannot afford a lamb, then he shall bring to the LORD as his guilt payment for the sin that he has committed two turtledoves or two young pigeons: one for a sin offering and one for a burnt offering.

**8** He shall bring them to the priest, who shall offer first the one for the sin offering. He shall pinch its head from its neck but shall not sever it.

**9** And he shall sprinkle some of the blood of the sin offering on the wall of the altar, while the rest of the blood shall be drained out at the base of the altar; it is a sin offering.

**10** And the second bird he shall offer as a burnt offering according to the prescribed rule. So the priest shall make atonement for him for the sin that he has committed, and he shall be forgiven.

**11** But if he cannot afford two turtledoves or two young pigeons, then he who sinned shall bring as his offering for the sin he committed a tenth of an ephah of fine flour as a sin offering. He shall put no oil on it and shall place no frankincense on it, for it is a sin offering.

**12** And he shall bring it to the priest, and the priest shall take a handful of it as its memorial portion and burn it on the altar, on the fire offerings of the LORD; it is a sin offering.

**13** So the priest shall make atonement for him for the sin that he has committed in any one of these things, and he shall be forgiven. The remainder shall belong to the priest, as with the grain offering.'"

**14** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**15** "If a person commits an act of unfaithfulness and sins unintentionally against the holy things of the LORD, he shall bring to the LORD as his guilt offering a ram without blemish from the flock, valued by you in silver shekels according to the shekel of the sanctuary, as a guilt offering.

**16** He shall also make restitution for the holy thing he has sinned against, and shall add a fifth to it and give it to the priest. And the priest shall make atonement for him with the ram of the guilt offering, and he shall be forgiven.

**17** And if a person sins, doing any one of the things the LORD has commanded not to be done, though he did not know it, then he incurs guilt and shall bear his iniquity.

**18** He shall bring to the priest a ram without blemish from the flock, valued by you, as a guilt offering. And the priest shall make atonement for him for the error that he committed unintentionally, though he did not know it, and he shall be forgiven.

**19** It is a guilt offering; he has indeed incurred guilt before the LORD."

---

## SOPHIA's Arbitration Notes

### Chapter Overview

Leviticus 5 divides into two distinct sections:

**Section A — Sin Offering (Chattat) for Four Cases (vv. 1–13):** Four situations in which a person incurs guilt (asham as guilt-state), with a graduated three-tier offering scale:
- Tier 1 (v. 6): Female lamb or female goat
- Tier 2 (vv. 7–10): Two birds — one sin offering, one burnt offering
- Tier 3 (vv. 11–13): One-tenth ephah of fine flour (no oil, no frankincense)

**Section B — Guilt Offering (Asham) for Two Cases (vv. 14–19):** Trespass against holy things (vv. 15–16, with monetary restitution plus one-fifth surcharge) and unknowing violation of a commandment (vv. 17–19, no restitution required).

---

### Key Arbitration Decisions

**1. "Purification offering" (PNEUMA) vs. "sin offering" (EMET, LOGOS) — chattat (vv. 6, 7, 9, 11, 12, 13)**

SOPHIA ruled in Leviticus 4 that chattat = "sin offering" throughout the SOPHIA Bible. PNEUMA again renders chattat as "purification offering" throughout vv. 6–13. This ruling is maintained: "sin offering" is the consensus rendering. "Purification offering" is a legitimate scholarly translation (Milgrom) and is noted in the Disagreement Log, but consistency across the Levitical corpus requires the established rendering.

**2. "Guilt payment" (EMET: ashamah) vs. "guilt offering" (LOGOS) vs. "penalty" (PNEUMA) — vv. 6–7**

The term in vv. 6–7 is אֲשָׁמָה (ashamah) — the abstract noun of the asham root functioning as the "reparation/penalty" the offender owes, distinct from the formal guilt offering (asham) of vv. 14–19. The actual sacrifice named is chattat (sin offering). EMET's "guilt payment" most accurately captures this bridging function without conflating it with the technical asham sacrifice. LOGOS's "guilt offering" risks creating confusion with Section B's distinct sacrifice type. PNEUMA's "penalty" is too vague. Consensus: "guilt payment" for ashamah in vv. 6–7, "guilt offering" reserved for asham in vv. 14–19.

**3. "Public adjuration to testify" (LOGOS) vs. "voice of an oath" (EMET) vs. "public call to testify" (PNEUMA) — v. 1**

The Hebrew is קוֹל אָלָה (qol alah, "voice of an oath/adjuration"). EMET preserves the literal idiom; LOGOS adds the forensic gloss "to testify" which correctly explains the legal context; PNEUMA paraphrases freely. Consensus adopts LOGOS's rendering ("a public adjuration to testify") as it is both contextually accurate and readable, while avoiding PNEUMA's loss of the oath/adjuration vocabulary. The phrase "hears a public adjuration to testify" captures the legal summons character of qol alah without over-literalizing.

**4. "Pinch its head" (EMET: malaq) vs. "wring its head" (LOGOS) vs. "wring its neck" (PNEUMA) — v. 8**

The Hebrew מָלַק (malaq) describes the distinctive bird-killing technique: the head is separated at the nape (mi-mul orpo) without complete severing. EMET's "pinch" is the most etymologically precise (malaq = to nip/pinch). LOGOS's "wring" is the standard English translation tradition (ESV, NASB, KJV). PNEUMA's "wring its neck" omits the explicit object ("its head") and the anatomical precision of mi-mul orpo. Consensus: "pinch its head from its neck" (EMET) — preserves both the action and anatomy of the phrase.

**5. "Wall of the altar" (EMET: qir ha-mizbeach) vs. "side of the altar" (LOGOS, PNEUMA) — v. 9**

The Hebrew קִיר הַמִּזְבֵּחַ (qir ha-mizbeach) means the wall or vertical face of the altar. EMET's "wall" is the literal rendering; LOGOS and PNEUMA use "side" (following ESV/NIV). "Wall" captures the vertical surface onto which the blood is sprinkled more precisely than "side" (which could suggest a lateral position rather than a face). Consensus: "wall of the altar" (EMET).

**6. "Memorial portion" (LOGOS) vs. "representative portion" (EMET) vs. "token portion" (PNEUMA) — azkarah (v. 12**

The Hebrew אַזְכָּרָה (azkarah) derives from the root z-k-r ("to remember"). "Memorial portion" (LOGOS, following ESV/NASB) is well-established in the translation tradition and accurately reflects both the etymology (remembrance) and the function (the handful that represents the whole). EMET's "representative portion" captures the functional sense. PNEUMA's "token portion" is accurate but deflates the covenantal weight of the remembrance concept. Consensus: "memorial portion" (LOGOS).

**7. "Commits an act of unfaithfulness" (LOGOS) vs. "commits unfaithfulness" (EMET) vs. "commits a breach of sacred trust" (PNEUMA) — ma'al (v. 15)**

The Hebrew מַעַל (ma'al) denotes a fundamental breach of sacred trust or faithfulness. LOGOS's "commits an act of unfaithfulness" and EMET's "commits unfaithfulness" both render ma'al with "unfaithfulness," which is established in the translation tradition (ESV). PNEUMA's "commits a breach of sacred trust" is a valid interpretive expansion but introduces "trust" language not present in the Hebrew. Consensus: "commits an act of unfaithfulness" (LOGOS) — includes the nominal "act" that reflects the substantive Hebrew construction, and uses "unfaithfulness" consistently.

**8. "Valued by you in silver shekels" (LOGOS) vs. "assessed by you" (EMET) vs. "assessed at the proper value" (PNEUMA) — be-erkekha (vv. 15, 18)**

EMET's "assessed by you" and LOGOS's "valued by you" both preserve the second-person address to the priestly assessor. PNEUMA's "assessed at the proper value" depersonalizes the priestly valuation role. Consensus: "valued by you" in v. 15 (full form: "valued by you in silver shekels according to the shekel of the sanctuary") following LOGOS; "valued by you" in v. 18 (LOGOS), omitting the redundant "in silver shekels" since the sanctuary standard is established. This preserves the priestly address and the monetary specificity.

**9. "He shall bear his iniquity" (EMET, LOGOS) vs. "bear the weight of their wrongdoing" (PNEUMA) — nasa avono (vv. 1, 17)**

The Hebrew וְנָשָׂא עֲוֹנוֹ (ve-nasa avono, "he shall bear his iniquity") is a standard forensic-theological expression. EMET and LOGOS agree on "shall bear his iniquity" (ESV, NASB, KJV tradition). PNEUMA's expansion ("bear the weight of their wrongdoing") adds interpretive color but loses the precise legal vocabulary of avon (iniquity). Consensus: "shall bear his iniquity" (EMET/LOGOS).

**10. Gender-inclusive pronouns (PNEUMA) vs. masculine singular (EMET, LOGOS)**

PNEUMA uses "they/their/them" throughout as gender-inclusive singular. EMET and LOGOS use masculine singular "he/his/him" following Hebrew grammatical convention (nefesh, while grammatically feminine in Hebrew, is idiomatically used with masculine verbal agreement). The SOPHIA Bible maintains grammatical fidelity to the Hebrew while acknowledging that nefesh is inclusive ("a person"). Consensus: render the subject as "a person" (inclusive) while using masculine singular pronouns in agreement with the Hebrew verbal forms (he/his/him), as in LOGOS and EMET. This is the established convention of the SOPHIA series.

**11. v. 14 — "And the LORD spoke" (EMET) vs. "The LORD spoke" (LOGOS) vs. "The LORD spoke to Moses:" (PNEUMA)**

The Hebrew has the standard narrative connector וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל-מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר (vayedabber YHWH el-Moshe lemor). The transitional waw (and) marks the shift to the guilt offering legislation. Consensus: "And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:" (EMET) — preserves the waw-consecutive connective that marks the structural division and the lemor ("saying") that introduces the direct speech.

**12. v. 19 — "He has indeed incurred guilt" (EMET, LOGOS) vs. "they were genuinely liable" (PNEUMA)**

The Hebrew אָשֹׁם אָשַׁם לַיהוָה (ashom asham la-YHWH) is the emphatic infinitive absolute construction: "guilty he has incurred guilt before the LORD." EMET and LOGOS render this with the conventional emphatic "has indeed incurred guilt." PNEUMA's "genuinely liable" captures the meaning but loses the rhetorical punch of the doubled root construction (a hallmark of Hebrew emphasis). Consensus: "he has indeed incurred guilt before the LORD" (EMET/LOGOS).

---

### Established Term Renderings (Leviticus Series, continued through ch. 5)

| Hebrew | SOPHIA Rendering | Chapter Established |
|--------|-----------------|-------------------|
| חַטָּאת (chattat) | sin offering | Lev 4 |
| אָשָׁם (asham) — sacrifice | guilt offering | Lev 5 |
| אֲשָׁמָה (ashamah) — guilt-liability noun | guilt payment | Lev 5 |
| מַעַל (ma'al) | unfaithfulness | Lev 5 |
| וְהִתְוַדָּה (ve-hitvaddah) | he shall confess | Lev 5 |
| חֲמִישִׁתוֹ (chamishitho) | a fifth | Lev 5 |
| שֶׁקֶל הַקֹּדֶשׁ (shekel ha-qodesh) | the shekel of the sanctuary | Lev 5 |
| אַזְכָּרָה (azkarah) | memorial portion | Lev 5 |
| וְנָשָׂא עֲוֹנוֹ (ve-nasa avono) | shall bear his iniquity | Lev 5 |
| מָלַק (malaq) | pinch (the head) | Lev 5 |
| קִיר הַמִּזְבֵּחַ (qir ha-mizbeach) | wall of the altar | Lev 5 |
| כַּמִּשְׁפָּט (ka-mishpat) | according to the prescribed rule | Lev 5 |
| עֹלָה (olah) | burnt offering | Lev 1 |
| מִנְחָה (minchah) | grain offering | Lev 2 |
| כִּפֶּר (kipper) | make atonement | Lev 1 |
| וְנִסְלַח (ve-nishlach) | and he shall be forgiven | Lev 4 |
| אִשֶּׁה (isheh) | fire offerings | Lev 1 |
| יהוה | the LORD | Genesis |

— SOPHIA
